content:
Member-only story

I avoided this migration for a year, convinced it would break everything. It took one afternoon.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated BY AIFor a year, every new Angular project I started used standalone components. Every old project? Still buried in NgModules.
I told myself the migration was risky. Too many moving parts. Better to wait for “later.”
Then I actually did it on a real production app. One afternoon. Zero production bugs. Here’s everything I wish I’d known before procrastinating that long.
The Problem
NgModules aren’t officially gone. But they’re no longer the default, the docs assume standalone, and new Angular features ship standalone-first.
Stay on NgModules and you accumulate:

• Boilerplate declarations and imports arrays that exist purely to register components

• Confusing import chains which module exports what, again?

• A growing gap between your codebase and current Angular tutorials, which all assume standalone
Why Developers Struggle With This
The hesitation isn’t irrational. It comes from real-sounding fears:
